---
title: "Project 1"
author: "Christian Bade"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(readr)
library(ggplot2)
```

## Overview

In the `data` directory of this project you will find the file from a paper published in *Nature Energy* titled [Natural gas savings in Germany during the 2022 energy crisis](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41560-023-01260-5). Here is the abstract of the article:

> Russia curbed its natural gas supply to Europe in 2021 and 2022, creating a grave energy crisis. This Article empirically estimates the crisis response of natural gas consumers in Germany—for decades, the largest export market for Russian gas. Using a multiple regression model, we estimate the response of small consumers, industry and power stations separately, controlling for the nonlinear temperature-heating relationship, seasonality and trends. We find significant and substantial gas savings for all consumer groups, but with differences in timing and size. For instance, industry started reducing consumption as early as September 2021, while small consumers saved substantially only since March 2022. Across all sectors, gas consumption during the second half of 2022 was 23% below the temperature-adjusted baseline. We discuss the drivers behind these savings and draw conclusions on their role in coping with the crisis.

Your job in this project falls into two categories:

1. A set of **tasks** that your group must complete exactly
2. A set of **objectives** that are more general in their approach.

## Tasks

### Task 1

* Load two files. To work in the console, use the `Session -> Set Working Directory -> To Source File Location`.
    * Call the first table `daily`: "./data/natural_gas_germany_daily.csv"
    * Call the second table `gas`: "./data/dutch_ttf_natural_gas.csv". Be sure to properly import the `Date` column.
    * Demonstrate that these have been loaded by showing the number of rows and columns in each table.
    
    
```{r}
daily <- read_csv("./data/natural_gas_germany_daily.csv")

gas <- read_csv("./data/dutch_ttf_natural_gas.csv", col_types = cols(Date = col_date()))

dim(daily)
nrow(daily)
ncol(daily)

dim(gas)
nrow(gas)
ncol(gas)
```
  
```{r}
daily <- read_csv("./data/natural_gas_germany_daily.csv")
gas <- read_csv("./data/dutch_ttf_natural_gas.csv") %>%
  mutate(Date = as.Date(Date, format = "%m/%d/%Y"))

dim(daily)
dim(gas)
```
### Task 2

* The data in `daily` are collected over days, with information on different types of natural gas consumption (`consumption_small`, `consumption_industry`, `consumption_power`). Provide summaries of typical values for each of these three types of consumption.

```{r}
daily %>%
  select(consumption_small, consumption_industry, consumption_power) %>%
  summary()
```

### Task 3

Answer some questions about the data in `daily`:

* How many weeks do the data cover?
* What is the percentage change in the `consumption_*` variables (that is the last day minus the first day divided by the first day)?
* What proportion of the days are marked as holidays?
* For each month in each year, what was the year-month combination with the lowest median `consumption_power` value?
   
```{r}
#weeks covered 
daily %>%
  summarise(
    weeks = as.numeric(difftime(max(date), min(date), units = "weeks"))
  )

```

```{r}
# percent change (last day - first day ) / first day
daily %>%
  summarise(
    small_change = (last(consumption_small) - first(consumption_small)) / first(consumption_small),
    industry_change = (last(consumption_industry) - first(consumption_industry)) / first(consumption_industry),
    power_change = (last(consumption_power) - first(consumption_power)) / first(consumption_power)
  )
```

```{r}
#proportion of holidays
daily %>%
  summarise(prop_holiday = mean(holiday == TRUE, na.rm = TRUE))

```

```{r}
daily %>%
  mutate(year_month = floor_date(date, "month")) %>%
  group_by(year_month) %>%
  summarise(median_power = median(consumption_power, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  arrange(median_power)
```
### Task 4

* The original paper aggregated the data to monthly means for each consumption type in `daily` and the `Price` column of `gas` to produce the following image:<br/>
![Original consumption by month graph](proj1fig1.png)<br/>
Produce plots that show the same information that is presented in this plot. Your plots do not have to have the same colors or markings, but we should be able to use them to compare the trends for the three price variables. 

```{r}
daily_prices_month <- daily %>%
  mutate(date_month = floor_date(date, "month")) %>%
  group_by(date_month) %>%
  summarise(
    household_idx = mean(price_households_idx, na.rm = TRUE),
    industry_idx = mean(price_industry_idx, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

gas_price_month <- gas %>%
  mutate(date_month = floor_date(Date, "month")) %>%
  group_by(date_month) %>%
  summarise(
    ttf_price = mean(Price, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

plot_data <- daily_prices_month %>%
  left_join(gas_price_month, by = "date_month")


scale_factor <- 4

ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = date_month)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = ttf_price, color = "Natural gas price TTF"), size = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = household_idx / scale_factor, color = "Natural gas price index for households DE"), size = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = industry_idx / scale_factor, color = "Natural gas price index for industry DE"),
            linetype = "dashed", size = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Natural gas price TTF (€ MWh⁻¹)",
    limits = c(0, 375),
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~ . * scale_factor, name = "Natural gas price indices (2015 = 100)")
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Natural gas price TTF" = "lightblue",
    "Natural gas price index for households DE" = "pink",
    "Natural gas price index for industry DE" = "pink"
  )) +
  labs(x = "Date", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "top", legend.direction = "vertical")
```

### Task 5

* Write a predicate function that returns true if any value in vector is missing. Use this function to find columns with missing values in the `daily` column. Create a plot or table that shows how often patterns of missingness occur: are all of the missing values in the same rows or are the various columns missing data in different ways?

```{r}
any_missing <- function(x) {
  any(is.na(x))
}

# Finding columns with missing values
cols_with_na <- sapply(daily, any_missing)
# cols_with_na

missing_matrix <- is.na(daily)

missing_pattern <- apply(missing_matrix, 1, function(row) paste(as.integer(row), collapse = ""))

## NOW count how many times each pattern happens
pattern_table <- table(missing_pattern)
pattern_table

```

"Missing no data" is vastly the most common. We can see that the rows missing data are largely missing data in the same way.

### Task 6

* Limit the `gas` table to days where the price exceeded the yearly median. Use the concept of [circular means](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Circular_mean) to compute the average day of the year when price exceeds the yearly median price. The `yday` function will likely be useful here. 

```{r}
med_price <- median(gas$Price, na.rm = TRUE)

gas_hi <- gas |> filter(Price > med_price) |> mutate(theta = 2 * pi * yday(Date) / 365)

mean_theta <- atan2(mean(sin(gas_hi$theta)), mean(cos(gas_hi$theta)))

if (mean_theta < 0) {
  mean_theta <- mean_theta + 2*pi
}

mean_yday <- (mean_theta / (2 * pi)) * 365


mean_yday
```


### Task 7

* Using the cut function, create two nominal variables from quantitative data in the `daily` dataset. Use these groups to summarize the data. Use arrange to show the smallest or largest values in these comparisons.

```{r}
daily_task7 <- daily %>%
  mutate(
    temp_category = cut(temperature,
                        breaks = 3,
                        labels = c("Cold", "Mild", "Warm")),
    industry_level = cut(consumption_industry,
                         breaks = quantile(consumption_industry, probs = seq(0, 1, 0.25), na.rm = TRUE),
                         include.lowest = TRUE,
                         labels = c("Very Low", "Low", "High", "Very High"))
  )

summary_task7 <- daily_task7 %>%
  # Filter out the rows where the cut function couldn't assign a category (the NAs)
  filter(!is.na(temp_category), !is.na(industry_level)) %>%
  group_by(temp_category, industry_level) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_small_cons = mean(consumption_small, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_power_cons = max(consumption_power, na.rm = TRUE),
    count = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(avg_small_cons))

print(summary_task7)
```


### Task 8

* There are several variables that pull out data by different industry (the `_idx` columns). Create a table for these columns using `select` and the `ends_with` function. Provide two different plots that show of the relations between these variables (you do not need to have all variables in each plot).

```{r}
# 1. Create a table using select and ends_with
industry_idx_table <- daily %>%
  select(date, ends_with("_idx"))
#ignores
# 2. Plot 1: Relationship between Manufacturing and Hospitality indices
ggplot(industry_idx_table, aes(x = manufacturing_idx, y = hospitality_idx)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "darkred") +
  labs(title = "Relationship between Manufacturing and Hospitality Indices",
       x = "Manufacturing Index",
       y = "Hospitality Index") +
  theme_minimal()

# 3. Plot 2: Time series comparison of all industry indices
industry_idx_long <- industry_idx_table %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = ends_with("_idx"), names_to = "Sector", values_to = "Index_Value")

ggplot(industry_idx_long, aes(x = date, y = Index_Value, color = Sector)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~Sector, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(title = "Trends in Industry Sector Indices Over Time",
       x = "Date",
       y = "Index Value") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```



## Objectives

# Tyger - Objectives 1, 2, and 3

### Objective 1

* Produce at least five more figures. For each figure, write a brief caption explaining the plot and what you have learned from the plot. Each figure should attempt to provide new insight into the data set not included elsewhere
    * A marginal distribution
    * A joint distribution
    * A plot of a summary measure such as a conditional mean
    * A plot using `facet_wrap` or `facet_grid`
    * A plot that shows seasonal effects before the crisis (September 2021 until October 2022)
  
```{r}
# 1. Marginal Distribution: Industry Consumption
ggplot(daily, aes(x = consumption_industry)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "steelblue", color = "white", bins = 30) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Industrial Gas Consumption",
       caption = "Figure 1: The distribution shows a slight bimodal tendency, reflecting shifts between high-production periods and crisis-driven reductions.") +
  theme_minimal()

# 2. Joint Distribution: Temperature vs. Household Consumption
ggplot(daily, aes(x = temperature, y = consumption_small)) +
  geom_bin2d(bins = 30) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c() +
  labs(title = "Density of Temperature vs. Household Consumption",
       caption = "Figure 2: There is a strong non-linear negative correlation; as temperature drops below 10°C, household gas demand spikes exponentially.") +
  theme_minimal()

# 3. Conditional Mean: Average Power Consumption by Weekday
daily %>%
  mutate(weekday = wday(date, label = TRUE)) %>%
  group_by(weekday) %>%
  summarise(mean_power = mean(consumption_power, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = weekday, y = mean_power, fill = weekday)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(title = "Mean Power Sector Gas Consumption by Weekday",
       caption = "Figure 3: Power sector consumption remains relatively stable across the week, with slight dips on weekends when industrial demand typically drops.") +
  theme_minimal()

# 4. Facet Plot: Industry Sector Indices
industry_idx_long %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Index_Value)) +
  geom_density(fill = "tomato", alpha = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~Sector, scales = "free") +
  labs(title = "Density Distributions of Industry Sector Indices",
       caption = "Figure 4: Different sectors show varying levels of volatility; some are tightly clustered while others show wider variance during the crisis.") +
  theme_minimal()

# 5. Seasonal Effects (Pre-Crisis to Crisis: Sept 2021 - Oct 2022)
daily %>%
  filter(date >= "2021-09-01" & date <= "2022-10-31") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = date, y = consumption_small)) +
  geom_line(color = "darkgreen") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Seasonal Household Consumption (Sept 2021 - Oct 2022)",
       caption = "Figure 5: Despite seasonal winter peaks, the 'loess' trend line shows the beginning of structural savings as the crisis deepened in 2022.") +
  theme_minimal()
```


### Objective 2

* Compare and contrast holidays and non-holidays for household energy consumption. Select 3 ways of comparing these groups. Provide at least one graph.

```{r}
# Statistical Summary
holiday_summary <- daily %>%
  group_by(holiday) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_cons = mean(consumption_small, na.rm = TRUE),
    med_cons = median(consumption_small, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_cons = sd(consumption_small, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

print(holiday_summary)

# Visual Comparison
ggplot(daily, aes(x = as.factor(holiday), y = consumption_small, fill = as.factor(holiday))) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title = "Household Consumption: Holidays vs. Normal Days",
    x = "Is Holiday? (False/True)",
    y = "Consumption (Small)",
    fill = "Holiday Status"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```


### Objective 3

* According to the paper, the gas crisis occurred between September 2021 until October 2022. Compare this period with the periods before and after on household and industrial consumption. Write a paragraph explaining your findings.

```{r}
daily_crisis <- daily %>%
  mutate(period = case_when(
    date < "2021-09-01" ~ "1. Pre-Crisis",
    date >= "2021-09-01" & date <= "2022-10-31" ~ "2. Crisis",
    date > "2022-10-31" ~ "3. Post-Crisis Peak"
  )) %>%
  filter(!is.na(period))

# Visualizing the shift in consumption patterns
ggplot(daily_crisis, aes(x = period, y = consumption_industry, fill = period)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.1, color = "black", outlier.shape = NA) +
  labs(title = "Industrial Gas Consumption Shift Across Crisis Periods",
       y = "Consumption (Industry)",
       x = "Time Period") +
  theme_minimal() +
  guides(fill = "none")
```


# Anela - Objectives 4 and 5

### Objective 4

* Explore the [documentation for ggplot](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/). Select one geometery and one `stat_` function we have not used before or use an option to a previously used geometry/stat with a new option. Write a short paragraph explaining what the plots show.

```{r}
raster_data <- daily %>%
  mutate(
    temp_bin = cut(temperature, breaks = 30),
    month = floor_date(date, "month")
  ) %>%
  group_by(month, temp_bin) %>%
  summarise(avg_consumption = mean(consumption_small, na.rm = TRUE),
            .groups = "drop")
ggplot(raster_data, aes(x = month, y = temp_bin, fill = avg_consumption)) +
  geom_raster() +
  labs(
    title = "Heatmap of Household Gas Consumption by Temperature and Time",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Temperature Bin",
    fill = "Avg Consumption"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

#done
```

```{r}
ggplot(daily, aes(x = temperature, y = consumption_small)) +
  stat_summary_bin(fun = mean, bins = 30, geom = "line", color = "blue") +
  labs(
    title = "Average Household Gas Consumption by Temperature",
    x = "Temperature",
    y = "Average Consumption"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

The first plot uses the geom geom_raster().This will show the average household's consumption of gas through time and temperature. By grouping the data into temperature ranges and months, the heatmap makes it easy to see that gas usage is higher during colder temperatures and winter months.This makes patterns easier to spot compared to a regular scatterplot.

The second plot uses the stat stat_summary_bin() to calculate the average gas consumption for different temperature ranges. It shows a downward trend, meaning gas consumption decreases as temperature increases, which supports the seasonal pattern seen in the first plot.

### Objective 4

```{r}
ggplot(daily, aes(x = solar_radiation)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, ) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Solar Radiation",
    x = "Solar Radiation",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
ggplot(daily, aes(x = temperature, y = solar_radiation)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Solar Radiation vs Temperature",
    x = "Temperature",
    y = "Solar Radiation"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

The first plot is a histogram of the distribution of solar radiation, which is right skewed with most values concentrated at lower levels and fewer high radiation days.

The scatter plot shows the relationship between solar radiation vs temperature, which has a positive  relationship indicating that temperatures that are higher are generally associated with higher solar radiation. This also demonstrates the seasonal patterns observed in the data where warmer days are associated with sunnier days,

### Objective 5

* Use `group_by` to summarize by a new feature of this data set not otherwise discussed in the tasks or objectives. What have you learned with these investigation?

```{r}
daily %>%
  mutate(day_of_week = wday(date, label = TRUE)) %>%
  group_by(day_of_week) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_small = mean(consumption_small, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_industry = mean(consumption_industry, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

daily %>%
  mutate(day_of_week = wday(date, label = TRUE)) %>%
  group_by(day_of_week) %>%
  summarise(avg_small = mean(consumption_small, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(day_of_week, avg_small)) +
  geom_col() +
  labs(
    title = "Average Household Gas Consumption by Day of Week",
    x = "Day of Week",
    y = "Average Consumption"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
```

When grouping the data by days of the week, average levels of consumption can be compared throughout different days of the week. The results indicate that there is very little variation between the average consumption grouped by different days of the week,  which suggests that household gas usage is driven more by weather and seasonal factors than by daily routines.

# Christian - Objectives 6 and 7

### Objective 6

* Based on your exploration of the data, suggest three questions that could be asked from these data or additional data that you can imagine. Be sure to explain why the previous plots or calculations indicates that this would be an interesting our useful exploration.

### Objective 7

* Write an abstract for your project. Briefly explain what you did, what you found, and why a potential reader should be interested in your research.
